event: The first human settlers arrive along Norway's coast as the ice sheets retreat after the last Ice Age.
Agriculture and the Neolithic way of life spread into southern Norway, supplementing hunting and fishing.
The Bronze Age brings metalworking, burial mounds, and extensive rock carvings to coastal Norway.
The Iron Age begins, with iron tools enabling expanded farming and the emergence of organized chieftaincies.
Norse raiders attack the monastery at Lindisfarne in England, marking the traditional start of the Viking Age.
Harald Fairhair wins the Battle of Hafrsfjord and begins unifying the petty kingdoms of Norway under one crown.
Norse settlers from Norway help found the Icelandic Commonwealth and its assembly, the Althing.
Olaf Tryggvason becomes king and aggressively promotes the Christianization of Norway.
King Olaf Tryggvason dies at the Battle of Svolder against a coalition of Scandinavian rivals.
King Olaf Haraldsson is killed at the Battle of Stiklestad and is later canonized as Saint Olaf, patron of Norway.
King Harald Hardrada dies at the Battle of Stamford Bridge in England, an event often seen as ending the Viking Age.
The death of King Sigurd the Crusader opens a century of civil wars over the Norwegian succession.
Haakon IV becomes king, ending the civil war era and inaugurating Norway's medieval golden age.
Iceland and Greenland submit to the Norwegian crown, expanding the kingdom across the North Atlantic.
The Black Death reaches Norway, killing roughly half the population and devastating the economy.
Norway enters a personal union with Denmark when Olaf II inherits both crowns.
The Kalmar Union unites Norway, Denmark, and Sweden under a single monarch.
Denmark imposes the Lutheran Reformation on Norway and reduces it to a province within the Danish realm.
Norway adopts a liberal constitution at Eidsvoll on May 17 but is forced into a union with Sweden after a brief war.
The first organized emigrant ship, the Restauration, sails to America, beginning mass Norwegian emigration.
Parliamentary government is established, shifting power from the king to the Storting.
Norway peacefully dissolves the union with Sweden and gains full independence, choosing Haakon VII as king.
Norwegian explorer Roald Amundsen leads the first expedition to reach the South Pole.
Norway grants women full voting rights, among the first nations in the world to do so.
Norway joins the League of Nations and gains sovereignty over Svalbard under the Spitsbergen Treaty.
Nazi Germany invades and occupies Norway; the king and government flee to London to lead resistance in exile.
Norway is liberated from German occupation and becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Norway becomes a founding member of the NATO military alliance.
Norway joins the European Free Trade Association as a founding member.
The Ekofisk field discovery launches Norway's transformative offshore oil and gas industry.
Norwegian voters reject membership in the European Economic Community in a national referendum.
Gro Harlem Brundtland becomes Norway's first female prime minister.
Norway establishes its Government Pension Fund to invest petroleum revenues for future generations.
Norway brokers the secret Oslo Accords between Israel and the Palestine Liberation Organization.
Norwegian voters again reject European Union membership, and Lillehammer hosts the Winter Olympics.
Anders Behring Breivik kills 77 people in bombings and a shooting in Oslo and on Utoya island.
Norway's sovereign wealth fund surpasses one trillion US dollars in value.
Norway leads the world in electric vehicle adoption, with EVs making up the majority of new car sales.
Norway becomes the first country where fully electric cars outnumber petrol cars on the road.
